statement: The Nordic countries vary considerably in their approach to post-employment restrictions for government officials, but several have stricter frameworks than Iceland. Denmark: The karantænelov (Act on Certain Restrictions on Ministers' and State Secretaries' Transition to the Private Sector, adopted 2019) imposes a mandatory 6-month general cooling-off period for ministers and state secretaries after leaving office, plus additional restrictions of up to 12 months if their new position relates to their former portfolio. An independent advisory board (Karantæneudvalget) assesses each case. Finland: The valtion virkamieslaki (State Civil Servants' Act, 750/1994, amended 2017) allows ministries to include up to 12-month cooling-off clauses in senior officials' employment contracts. The practice is not universal but has become increasingly common for permanent secretaries (kansliapäälliköt) and directors-general. A 2020 government resolution recommends such clauses for all senior positions. Sweden: Sweden does not have a general cooling-off law for ministers, but the Nämnden för prövning av statsråds och statssekreterares övergångsrestriktioner (Board for Review of Transition Restrictions for Ministers and State Secretaries), established in 2018, can impose transition restrictions of up to 12 months when ministers or state secretaries move to the private sector. Norway (non-EU but relevant as an EEA comparator): The karanteneloven (Quarantine Act, 2015) is the strictest in the Nordic region, imposing up to 6 months of quarantine (karantene) plus up to 12 months of restricted activity (saksforbud) for ministers, state secretaries, and political advisors. An independent committee (Karantenenemnda) assesses each transition. By comparison, Iceland has no mandatory cooling-off period, no independent review body, and only non-binding guidance in the ministers' ethics code.
source_name: Danish Karantænelov (2019); Finnish Valtion virkamieslaki 750/1994; Swedish SOU 2017:3; Norwegian Karanteneloven (2015); OECD Post-Public Employment report (2022)
caveats: The effectiveness of Nordic cooling-off regimes varies. Norway's system is considered the most robust due to mandatory application and an active independent committee. Denmark's 2019 law is relatively new and has had limited case law. Finland's approach is contractual rather than statutory, meaning coverage depends on individual employment agreements. Sweden's board can only recommend restrictions, though recommendations have been followed in practice. Direct comparison with Iceland is complicated by Iceland's much smaller government apparatus — the potential revolving-door pool is smaller, but so are the degrees of separation between government and the private sector, which arguably makes the risk higher per capita.

related_entries.statement: Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum nr. 13/2005 (Act on Prevention of Conflicts of Interest no. 13/2005) is Iceland's primary statute governing conflicts of interest for officials in the central government (Stjórnarráðið). The act applies to ministers, permanent secretaries (ráðuneytisstjórar), and other senior officials. Key provisions include: (1) mandatory disclosure of financial interests, board memberships, and secondary employment upon taking office (Article 4); (2) obligation to recuse from decisions where personal financial or other interests create a conflict (Article 5); (3) a prohibition on officials using insider information obtained through their position for personal gain (Article 6). The act was strengthened in 2012 alongside the broader overhaul of the Government Offices Act (lög um Stjórnarráð Íslands nr. 115/2011). Notably, the act does NOT contain any post-employment restrictions or cooling-off periods — once an official leaves office, the act's obligations cease. This gap was flagged in GRECO's Fourth Evaluation Round report on Iceland (GrecoEval4Rep(2017)5), which recommended that Iceland introduce post-employment restrictions for persons with top executive functions. As of early 2026, no such restrictions have been enacted into law.

Iceland has an established legal framework for managing conflicts of interest in government. Key instruments include: (1) Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum í Stjórnarráðinu (Act on Prevention of Conflicts of Interest in Government Offices), which requires disclosure of financial interests and imposes restrictions on officials' participation in decisions where they have personal interests; (2) Siðareglur ráðherra (Code of Ethics for Ministers), which establishes standards of conduct for cabinet members; (3) General administrative law principles on disqualification (vanhæfi) under the Administrative Procedures Act (stjórnsýslulög nr. 37/1993). However, Iceland does not have a post-employment cooling-off period requirement for ministers or senior officials — there is no mandatory waiting period before former officials can take positions in the private sector or with international organisations. This gap has been highlighted in international assessments, including GRECO (Group of States against Corruption) evaluation reports.

Many Western democracies have stricter post-employment (revolving door) restrictions than Iceland for senior government officials. Examples include: France requires a 3-year cooling-off period for senior officials before joining companies they supervised, enforced by the Haute Autorité pour la Transparence de la Vie Publique (HATVP); Canada imposes a 2-year post-employment restriction for ministers and 1 year for senior officials, enforced by the Office of the Conflict of Interest and Ethics Commissioner; the European Commission imposes a 2-year cooling-off period for departing Commissioners (extended to 3 years for the Commission President), during which they must notify and receive approval for any new professional activities; the United Kingdom has an Advisory Committee on Business Appointments (ACOBA) that reviews post-employment moves by ministers and senior officials, typically recommending 6–24 month waiting periods (though compliance is voluntary). By contrast, Iceland has no mandatory cooling-off period for ministers or senior officials, a gap highlighted in GRECO evaluations.
